analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Aljamain Sterling -- Style & Profile

- Sterling built his reputation as an elite grappler and suffocating clinch fighter, but his last three fights tell a different story: he's averaging 0.65 significant strikes per minute and his takedown output has cratered to roughly one per 15 minutes in the Evloev and Zalal fights. He's winning by doing very little offensively, which works until it doesn't.
- His strike defense (59% career) and his ability to control range and pace have been his real weapons lately — he's not outworking opponents, he's out-positioning them. Against Ortega and Zalal, he won primarily by controlling the cage and avoiding damage rather than landing meaningful offense.
- The KO loss to O'Malley in 2022 is the one blemish that carries forward: a right hand ended the fight in 51 seconds of Round 2, and at 36 years old in a weight class full of young, fast strikers, that vulnerability deserves attention. He has not been badly hurt since, but he also hasn't been in many firefights.
- His submission game (0.6 attempts per 15 minutes) has effectively disappeared from his recent fights — zero submission attempts in his last three. He's evolved into a grappling-threat-as-deterrent fighter: the threat keeps opponents honest, but he rarely pulls the trigger.
- At 36, he's at the age where the margins start closing on fighters who win on craft and positioning rather than athleticism. His output in the Evloev loss (0.2 SLpM, 27.3% accuracy) and the first Zalal fight (0.32 SLpM, 33.3% accuracy) suggests his offensive ceiling has dropped significantly from his championship prime.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Youssef Zalal -- Style & Profile

- Zalal is a switch-stance fighter who has reinvented himself over the last two years — he went from a striker who lost decisions to a grappling-based submission threat who has finished four of his last six opponents by submission before this fight card. His career sub average (1.3 per 15 minutes) understates how active he's become on the ground.
- His striking volume is genuinely low (2.78 SLpM career, and he threw essentially nothing in his last two fights before the first Sterling bout), which means his path to winning almost always runs through takedowns and scrambles. He's not trying to out-box anyone — he's trying to get the fight to the mat.
- The problem exposed in the first fight: he went zero-for-zero on takedowns against Sterling in 25 minutes, and his striking output collapsed to 0.16 SLpM with 25% accuracy. When his grappling is neutralized, his offense becomes nearly nonexistent.
- His strike defense is legitimately strong at 64% career — he moves well, angles off, and doesn't stand in front of punches. Combined with only 1.79 strikes absorbed per minute, he's hard to hurt cleanly even when he's losing.
- At 29, he's on the right side of the age curve and his recent form (four submission wins in a five-fight win streak) suggests genuine momentum and confidence. His win over Josh Emmett — a historically tough, durable fighter — demonstrated that his grappling works against high-level opposition.
- His takedown defense (55% career) is a real liability. If Sterling can establish consistent wrestling, Zalal can be controlled on the bottom, and Sterling's top game historically generates enough ground and pound to win rounds.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have the answer to this matchup once — Sterling won a unanimous decision across five rounds, holding Zalal to zero takedowns and a combined 25% striking accuracy over a full main event. The question for a rematch is what, if anything, has changed. Sterling showed that his positional awareness and takedown defense (44% career, which actually understates how well he stuffed Zalal specifically) are enough to neutralize Zalal's entire game plan. Zalal's path to victory runs through takedowns and scrambles, and when that road is blocked, he essentially has no offense. That's not a good formula against a fighter who wins by outworking you in control time.

The physical picture slightly favors Zalal — he's an inch taller, has a marginal reach advantage, fights out of a switch stance that can create alignment problems, and is seven years younger. That age gap matters at 36; Sterling's offensive output has dropped noticeably since his championship run. But youth and stance don't compensate for the fact that Zalal's grappling was completely shut down for 25 minutes in their first meeting. For the rematch to go differently, Zalal either needs a new wrinkle in how he sets up takedowns, or he needs to become a genuine striking threat — and nothing in his last three fights suggests the latter is coming. His 0.0 SLpM against Emmett (a win, by submission in Round 1) shows he's fully committed to the grappling path.

Sterling's challenge is simpler: do enough to win rounds without taking risks. At his current output levels, he's winning fights by not losing them — minimal striking, enough grappling threat to control cage space, no major defensive lapses. That formula beat Zalal once, and there's no structural reason it fails again. The net intangible picture slightly favors Zalal on age alone, but Sterling has the experience and proven blueprint for this specific matchup.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Aljamain Sterling wins by unanimous decision, Round 5**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly what happened the first time, and nothing in the available data suggests Zalal has developed new tools to change the outcome. Sterling neutralizes the takedown game, controls cage position, and does just enough striking to take rounds. Zalal's offense goes quiet the moment his grappling is stifled, and Sterling has already proven he can stifle it for a full 25 minutes.

**#2 -- Youssef Zalal wins by submission, Rounds 2-4**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Zalal's submission finishing has been his identity in this run — four finishes in five wins — and Sterling's sub defense in his recent fights leans more on positioning than active scrambling. If Zalal finds a takedown in a scramble or off the cage at some point in the middle rounds and gets to a back-take or body triangle position, he has the submission instincts to convert. It's a low-probability path, but it's the most realistic way the upset happens.

**#3 -- Zalal wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: For Zalal to outpoint Sterling over five rounds, he needs to land cleaner strikes in exchanges and generate more control time than his first fight showed he's capable of against this opponent. Sterling's positional savvy makes it very hard for Zalal to establish the dominance he'd need on the scorecards, but a Zalal who comes out with a more aggressive striking game from the switch stance could potentially steal rounds if Sterling's output stays as low as it has been.

**#4 -- Sterling wins by TKO/Stoppage**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Sterling's finishing rate has essentially flatlined — one TKO and one submission in his last ten fights, both several years ago. At his current offensive output levels, a finish requires Zalal to make a major mistake and get badly hurt. Zalal's strike defense is solid enough and his chin hasn't been tested by a meaningful knockout shot at featherweight, making this the least likely scenario that ends with Sterling's hand raised.
analysis_sections.betting:
This is a rematch where we have a clean first-fight result, significant line movement to process, and a five-round main event format. Let's work through it.

The line has moved dramatically — Sterling opened at -155 and is now sitting around +105, while Zalal has moved from +115 to around +135. That's a massive shift toward Zalal. This is not reverse line movement; the line has moved toward Zalal as the underdog, meaning sharp money has hammered him enough to flip Sterling from a moderate favorite to a slight underdog. That is a significant signal and demands serious weight.

**The case for the pick:**

Sterling won the first fight, but the line flip suggests the market — and presumably sharp money with more information than the public — believes the rematch sets up differently. At +105 on Sterling, you're essentially getting a slight plus-money price on the fighter who already beat this opponent once. That's the pull. Sterling's formula is proven against Zalal, and his defensive profile (59% strike defense, effective cage work) doesn't disappear in a rematch.

However, the sharp money moving this aggressively toward Zalal is a signal that can't be ignored. There could be camp intel, stylistic adjustments, or physical condition information baked into that move that isn't visible in the box scores. At 36, Sterling is also on the wrong side of the age curve, and his last three fight performances show a fighter operating at a fraction of his peak offensive output.

**The honest read:**

Sterling at +105 has real value relative to the first fight's outcome, but the size and direction of that line movement creates genuine uncertainty. A pick here requires either trusting the proven blueprint or trusting that sharp money knows something we don't.

Given the dramatic line movement toward Zalal, Sterling at +105 qualifies as a lean — the price is right for the fighter with the proven edge in this specific matchup, but the market signal introduces enough doubt to keep the stake modest.

**Recommendation: LEAN — Sterling at +105**
Confidence: 56%
Stake: 1 unit
Target line: Sterling up to -115 (beyond that, the value thins enough to pass)

**Data note:** Both fighters' intangible data from Tapology is unavailable, so weight-miss history, camp changes, and short-notice flags cannot be assessed. That gap keeps this at a lean rather than a play.

**Market read:** Sterling opened -155 and has moved to approximately +105 — a massive shift toward Zalal. This is not reverse line movement in the technical sense (the line moved toward the underdog), but the magnitude of that move is a sharp signal worth respecting. The stat-based read favors Sterling; the market move favors Zalal. Those signals conflict, which appropriately reduces confidence.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Live odds are unavailable for exact cross-book comparison. No arbitrage math can be computed. Take the Sterling lean at the best available line around +105 if the price holds; if it's moved to -110 or shorter by fight time, pass.

**Props:** The most interesting prop angle is "fight goes to decision." The first meeting went the full 25 minutes, both fighters' offense is historically low in this matchup (Sterling 0.32 SLpM, Zalal 0.16 SLpM in fight one), and neither has shown finishing threat in this specific pairing. If "goes to decision" is available at a price implying less than 65% probability, it has value — the structure of this rematch strongly favors another five-round war of attrition.

**Flip conditions:** If Zalal's camp has credible reports of significant new striking development, reconsider. If Sterling has any undisclosed injury from the first fight (layoff data shows 1 day, suggesting a same-card context), downgrade to no bet. If the line moves to Sterling -130 or beyond before the fight, the value is gone on that side.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Joselyne Edwards -- Style & Profile

- Edwards is an orthodox striker who has reinvented herself over the past year. Her last three wins came by three different methods — TKO, submission, and decision — which tells you she's comfortable wherever the fight goes, even if her grappling output in those fights was nearly nonexistent statistically.
- Her volume is highly situational. Against Cachoeira she averaged nearly 11 significant strikes per minute; against Dumont in the first fight she averaged 0.4. She doesn't impose pace — she responds to what's in front of her, which can look either tactically smart or dangerously passive depending on the night.
- Her striking accuracy sits at 53% for her career, which is legitimately elite at 135 lbs. When she does pull the trigger, she connects. The issue is getting her to commit to being the aggressor.
- She has a real chin. Despite taking 3.26 significant strikes per minute over her career, she has never been stopped by strikes in the UFC. She's been hurt and kept fighting, which is a meaningful data point at bantamweight.
- At 30, she's in her physical prime and holds a 5-year age advantage over Dumont, plus a 3-inch reach edge. Those margins don't guarantee anything but they are structural advantages that compound over three rounds.
- She beat Dumont already — just three months ago — by unanimous decision. The question isn't whether she can win this fight; it's whether the rematch changes anything.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Norma Dumont -- Style & Profile

- Dumont is one of the most technically conservative fighters in the UFC women's bantamweight division. She has gone to decision in all eight of her last eight wins, never finishing an opponent by strikes or submission in her entire UFC career. Her game plan is to accumulate volume, control cage position, and survive close rounds on the scorecards.
- Her strike defense is excellent at 64% career — she doesn't absorb much, and her 2.33 strikes absorbed per minute is among the lowest in the division. She is genuinely hard to hurt, but she also doesn't hurt people herself, and that's a structural ceiling on her ceiling in competitive fights.
- At 35, she's past the age where female fighters typically maintain their sharpest reflexes and reaction time. Her last-three splits show a steady decrease in output: 2.13 SLpM against Aldana, 1.13 against Vieira, and just 0.4 against Edwards in their first meeting. She is getting quieter as fights go on and as opponents get more comfortable with her.
- Her takedown game on paper looks decent — 54% accuracy, 71% defense — but she has landed zero takedowns in each of her last three fights. Whatever wrestling she has is not being converted into actual cage control or position changes in recent bouts.
- She's never been knocked out in her career, but she also doesn't put herself in danger. It's hard to evaluate her chin because she fights so safely. What's clear is she absorbs very little, which protects the unknown.
- Dumont is a pure point-fighter who needs the judges to go her way. When opponents are physically bigger, younger, and more dangerous on the feet, she becomes reliant on early-round efficiency to bank scores before fatigue or physicality tips the balance.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch of a fight that happened three months ago, and the result was a clear unanimous decision for Edwards. The structural reasons for that outcome haven't changed: Edwards is younger, taller, longer-armed, and more multi-dimensional. Dumont's entire game plan is to out-volume opponents on the feet and win on the scorecards, but she produced just 0.4 significant strikes per minute against Edwards in their first fight — the exact same number Edwards put up. Neither fighter was particularly active, but Edwards was more effective when she did engage, and the judges saw it that way all three rounds.

The key variable in any rematch is whether Dumont shows up with a different game plan, and there's not much evidence her toolkit has expanded. She has never attempted a submission in her UFC career, her takedown attempts have dried up entirely in recent fights, and she has no finishing ability to threaten with. Her path back to a win requires her to be noticeably more active and precise on the feet than she was in fight one — outworking Edwards by volume across all three rounds — which would be a significant departure from a fighter whose output has actually been trending downward. Edwards, by contrast, has the tools to win multiple ways: she can strike, she can mix in the clinch, and she has the submission win over Cornolle fresh in her memory if the fight ever hits the mat. The reach advantage also means Edwards can dictate range without being forced into Dumont's preferred pocket.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Edwards — she's five years younger, holds a clear physical size advantage with the reach differential, and is riding three consecutive wins. Neither fighter has significant camp or layoff concerns given the one-day layoff figure is almost certainly a data artifact from the fight being a same-event rematch booking. The main uncertainty is whether a motivated Dumont at 35, knowing exactly what went wrong, can execute differently. That's a real but historically unlikely reversal given her career-long pattern.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight went exactly this way, and neither fighter's tendencies have changed. Edwards controls range with her reach advantage, lands cleaner when she engages, and does enough across three rounds to take two or all three on the cards. Dumont's inability to generate volume against Edwards — 0.4 SLpM in their first meeting — makes it very difficult for her to bank rounds consistently.

**#2 -- Norma Dumont wins by split or unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Dumont has made a career of winning close fights on the scorecards, and judges in women's bantamweight have been notoriously unpredictable. If Dumont is more active early and banks Rounds 1 and 2 before either fighter fades, the cards could flip. Her 64% strike defense limits Edwards's ability to impose damage, so the scorecards become the only real battleground.

**#3 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by stoppage**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Edwards has shown she can finish — the Cachoeira TKO and the Cornolle submission in her recent run are both real. If Dumont's output drops further and she becomes static, Edwards could land something clean. That said, Dumont fights safely and rarely puts herself in dangerous positions, so a finish would require a meaningful momentum shift that her style is specifically designed to avoid.
analysis_sections.betting:
**Recommendation: LEAN — Joselyne Edwards**

The case for Edwards is straightforward: she already beat Dumont clearly three months ago, she holds structural advantages in age, reach, and finishing ability, and Dumont's output against her in fight one was as low as Edwards's own — which means Dumont can't out-busy her. The rematch dynamic occasionally produces a reversal, but Dumont's toolkit hasn't expanded, and her output trends have been moving in the wrong direction across her last three fights.

The problem is the line. Edwards opened at +170 and has shortened to +165. Dumont opened at +195 on her side — meaning the books opened this as an Edwards fight — and Dumont is now priced around -200. That's a massive 395-cent total swing in Dumont's favor without confirmed sharp reverse line movement driving it. The market has swung hard to Dumont on what appears to be a rematch narrative and possibly betting action that isn't clearly sharp. There is no reverse line movement indicator — the line moved toward Dumont — but the magnitude of the move from a position where Edwards was the implied favorite at open makes the current Edwards price genuinely interesting.

At +165, Edwards's implied probability is roughly 37.7%. De-vigged against Dumont's -200 (implied 66.7%), the combined implied is 104.4%, and the true de-vigged probability on Edwards lands around 36.1%. If you believe Edwards wins this fight at somewhere between 50% and 55% based on the first fight result and the structural advantages, there is real line value on her as the underdog. That's a meaningful gap between what the market is saying and what the data from their actual fight suggests.

**Pick: Joselyne Edwards +165**
Assessed win probability: approximately 52–55%
Market true probability (de-vigged): approximately 36%
Stake: 1.5 units
Target line: The bet holds value down to approximately +120. Below that, the edge compresses inside noise.

Both fighters are assessed at a medium confidence level given the last-three data is heavily dominated by a single prior meeting and Dumont's pre-fight intangibles are not fully available. Neither fighter produces high-confidence projections in isolation, which means this is a lean rather than a strong play.

Market data: Edwards opened as the implied favorite around +170/Dumont +195. The market has moved significantly to Dumont at -200 with no confirmed sharp reverse line movement. The move is notable in magnitude but not confirmed as sharp money — treat with caution.

**Hedge verdict: NO ARB.** Best available lines (Edwards +165 / Dumont -200) combine to an implied total of approximately 104.4%, which is above 100% — no arbitrage exists. Take the value pick on Edwards at the best available line.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight goes to decision" prop aligns well with both fighters' profiles — Dumont has gone to decision in every UFC win, and Edwards's last three wins include a decision. If the decision prop is available at YES around -140 or better (implied roughly 58%), it may carry value given the near-certainty of this fight going to the scorecards absent a significant momentum change. De-vig the prop market before placing.

**Conditions that would change this read:** If Dumont has made documented camp or coaching changes specifically designed to address her output problem against Edwards, that would warrant moving toward no bet. If Edwards is dealing with an undisclosed injury that explains the line move, that would be a hard pass. If the line continues moving to Dumont -220 or beyond without a clear news catalyst, it may signal something the public market knows that isn't reflected in the available stats — at that point, reducing stake or passing entirely is appropriate.

- At 30, she's in her physical prime and holds a 5-year age advantage over Dumont, plus a 3-inch reach edge. Those margins don't guarantee anything but they are structural advantages that compound over three rounds.
- She beat Dumont already — just three months ago — by unanimous decision. The question isn't whether she can win this fight; it's whether the rematch changes anything.
<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Norma Dumont -- Style & Profile

- Dumont is one of the most technically conservative fighters in the UFC women's bantamweight division. She has gone to decision in all eight of her last eight wins, never finishing an opponent by strikes or submission in her entire UFC career. Her game plan is to accumulate volume, control cage position, and survive close rounds on the scorecards.
- Her strike defense is excellent at 64% career — she doesn't absorb much, and her 2.33 strikes absorbed per minute is among the lowest in the division. She is genuinely hard to hurt, but she also doesn't hurt people herself, and that's a structural ceiling on her ceiling in competitive fights.
- At 35, she's past the age where female fighters typically maintain their sharpest reflexes and reaction time. Her last-three splits show a steady decrease in output: 2.13 SLpM against Aldana, 1.13 against Vieira, and just 0.4 against Edwards in their first meeting. She is getting quieter as fights go on and as opponents get more comfortable with her.
- Her takedown game on paper looks decent — 54% accuracy, 71% defense — but she has landed zero takedowns in each of her last three fights. Whatever wrestling she has is not being converted into actual cage control or position changes in recent bouts.
- She's never been knocked out in her career, but she also doesn't put herself in danger. It's hard to evaluate her chin because she fights so safely. What's clear is she absorbs very little, which protects the unknown.
- Dumont is a pure point-fighter who needs the judges to go her way. When opponents are physically bigger, younger, and more dangerous on the feet, she becomes reliant on early-round efficiency to bank scores before fatigue or physicality tips the balance.
<!--END-->

<!--HEAD2HEAD-->
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch of a fight that happened three months ago, and the result was a clear unanimous decision for Edwards. The structural reasons for that outcome haven't changed: Edwards is younger, taller, longer-armed, and more multi-dimensional. Dumont's entire game plan is to out-volume opponents on the feet and win on the scorecards, but she produced just 0.4 significant strikes per minute against Edwards in their first fight — the exact same number Edwards put up. Neither fighter was particularly active, but Edwards was more effective when she did engage, and the judges saw it that way all three rounds.

The key variable in any rematch is whether Dumont shows up with a different game plan, and there's not much evidence her toolkit has expanded. She has never attempted a submission in her UFC career, her takedown attempts have dried up entirely in recent fights, and she has no finishing ability to threaten with. Her path back to a win requires her to be noticeably more active and precise on the feet than she was in fight one — outworking Edwards by volume across all three rounds — which would be a significant departure from a fighter whose output has actually been trending downward. Edwards, by contrast, has the tools to win multiple ways: she can strike, she can mix in the clinch, and she has the submission win over Cornolle fresh in her memory if the fight ever hits the mat. The reach advantage also means Edwards can dictate range without being forced into Dumont's preferred pocket.

The net intangible picture slightly favors Edwards — she's five years younger, holds a clear physical size advantage with the reach differential, and is riding three consecutive wins. Neither fighter has significant camp or layoff concerns given the one-day layoff figure is almost certainly a data artifact from the fight being a same-event rematch booking. The main uncertainty is whether a motivated Dumont at 35, knowing exactly what went wrong, can execute differently. That's a real but historically unlikely reversal given her career-long pattern.

<!--END-->

<!--ENDINGS-->
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by unanimous decision**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight went exactly this way, and neither fighter's tendencies have changed. Edwards controls range with her reach advantage, lands cleaner when she engages, and does enough across three rounds to take two or all three on the cards. Dumont's inability to generate volume against Edwards — 0.4 SLpM in their first meeting — makes it very difficult for her to bank rounds consistently.

**#2 -- Norma Dumont wins by split or unanimous decision**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Dumont has made a career of winning close fights on the scorecards, and judges in women's bantamweight have been notoriously unpredictable. If Dumont is more active early and banks Rounds 1 and 2 before either fighter fades, the cards could flip. Her 64% strike defense limits Edwards's ability to impose damage, so the scorecards become the only real battleground.

**#3 -- Joselyne Edwards wins by stoppage**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Edwards has shown she can finish — the Cachoeira TKO and the Cornolle submission in her recent run are both real. If Dumont's output drops further and she becomes static, Edwards could land something clean. That said, Dumont fights safely and rarely puts herself in dangerous positions, so a finish would require a meaningful momentum shift that her style is specifically designed to avoid.
<!--END-->

<!--BETTING-->
**Recommendation: LEAN — Joselyne Edwards**

The case for Edwards is straightforward: she already beat Dumont clearly three months ago, she holds structural advantages in age, reach, and finishing ability, and Dumont's output against her in fight one was as low as Edwards's own — which means Dumont can't out-busy her. The rematch dynamic occasionally produces a reversal, but Dumont's toolkit hasn't expanded, and her output trends have been moving in the wrong direction across her last three fights.

The problem is the line. Edwards opened at +170 and has shortened to +165. Dumont opened at +195 on her side — meaning the books opened this as an Edwards fight — and Dumont is now priced around -200. There is no reverse line movement indicator — the line moved toward Dumont — but the magnitude of the move from a position where Edwards was the implied favorite at open makes the current Edwards price genuinely interesting.

At +165, Edwards's implied probability is roughly 37.7%. De-vigged against Dumont's -200 (implied 66.7%), the combined implied is 104.4%, and the true de-vigged probability on Edwards lands around 36.1%. If you believe Edwards wins this fight at somewhere between 50% and 55% based on the first fight result and the structural advantages, there is real line value on her as the underdog. That's a meaningful gap between what the market is saying and what the data from their actual fight suggests.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Rafa Garcia -- Style & Profile

- Garcia is primarily a pressure wrestler who wins by controlling where the fight happens. His 3.22 takedowns per 15 minutes and 77% takedown defense give him a clear structural identity: he puts opponents on their back, keeps them there, and grinds out rounds. Seven of his last ten wins have gone to decision, and that's not an accident — it's his game plan every time.

- His striking output has been remarkably low in his last three fights. He's landing under 1.8 significant strikes per minute across all three, compared to his career average of 4.28. That divergence tells you he's not trying to win striking exchanges — he's using his hands to set up takedowns and clinch entries, not to hurt anyone.

- He stopped Jared Gordon by TKO in Round 3 of their September 2025 bout, which is the most notable finish on his recent résumé. Gordon was knocked out in the final round, suggesting Garcia's stoppages come from accumulated work late — grinding opponents down rather than cracking them with a single shot.

- His takedown defense at 77% is the single most important number in this matchup. It means he can keep the fight standing if he wants, or take it down when he doesn't. That kind of two-way grappling control is what makes pressure wrestlers difficult to solve.

- Garcia already has a win over Hernandez — the most recent fight in both men's records, decided by unanimous decision in April 2026. That prior result matters: Garcia clearly solved the puzzle once, and Hernandez has to show he's made meaningful adjustments.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Alexander Hernandez -- Style & Profile

- Hernandez is a striker who has generated real knockouts in his recent fights — he stopped Chase Hooper inside Round 1 and Diego Ferreira in Round 2. But his career numbers (40% striking accuracy, 59% strike defense, 4.45 significant strikes absorbed per minute) suggest he lives in close, messy exchanges where he takes as much as he gives.

- His takedown defense is a legitimate vulnerability at 68%, and in his most recent fight — the loss to Garcia — he managed zero takedowns himself. Hernandez is not a grappler, and when the fight goes to the ground, he doesn't have much of a solution. His submission attempts average is essentially zero, and he's been submitted before (Renato Moicano, Round 2).

- The KO wins over Hooper and Ferreira came against opponents who were either size-compromised or declining, and Hooper in particular is not known for his durability. Those wins confirm Hernandez has genuine punching power, but the competition quality limits how much weight to give them.

- In the rematch with Garcia, Hernandez landed just 0.53 significant strikes per minute and connected on only 29.6% of what he threw. That's not a close loss where he competed on even terms — that's a performance where he couldn't find a rhythm at all, suggesting Garcia's pressure and grappling genuinely shut him down.

- At 33 years old with a 68% takedown defense and a history that includes four losses in a six-fight stretch (2020–2022), Hernandez is a fighter whose ceiling is reasonably well-established. He's capable of finishing fights, but he needs to keep them standing and needs clean exchanges to do it.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This fight has a clear structural dynamic: Garcia is the wrestler who controls the pace and location of the fight, and Hernandez is the striker who needs clean space to land. The master variable here is Hernandez's 68% takedown defense — below the threshold where a striker can confidently stay upright against a dedicated wrestler like Garcia. In their first meeting, Garcia exploited that exact gap, and Hernandez had no answer for it over three rounds. Garcia doesn't need to finish — he just needs to keep Hernandez against the fence, mix in takedowns, and bank rounds, which is precisely what he did in April 2026.

Hernandez's path to winning this rematch runs through keeping the fight at range and making Garcia pay for every level change. He has the 2-inch reach advantage, and if he can get Garcia respecting the jab and circling, he might be able to land the kind of clean shots that led to his KOs against Hooper and Ferreira. The problem is that Garcia's pressure style doesn't give opponents clean range to work from — he crowds, smothers, and ties up, which is the environment where Hernandez is least effective. His striking output in the first fight (0.53 significant strikes per minute) was so suppressed that it borders on being stylistically neutralized.

The intangible of a direct rematch immediately after the original fight is relevant here. Hernandez needs to show he's made a meaningful adjustment — a different game plan, better answers for Garcia's pressure — because the default outcome of this fight, based on everything available, looks a lot like the first one. Garcia enters as the fighter who has already solved this matchup. The line moving sharply toward Hernandez (from +105 to -150) is notable, but there's no confirmed reverse line movement — the market has shifted, and that's worth acknowledging, but it doesn't override the structural read that Garcia controls this fight.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Rafa Garcia wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: Garcia's blueprint from their first meeting — pressure, takedowns, clinch control — is the same blueprint he'll run back. With Hernandez unable to stop takedowns at a high rate and no demonstrated counter-adjustment to Garcia's wrestling, the most probable outcome is another three rounds of Garcia controlling position and banking judges' rounds on volume and grappling work.

**#2 -- Alexander Hernandez wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Hernandez's most realistic path involves landing a clean shot early, before Garcia's pressure fully takes hold. His KO victories over Hooper and Ferreira both came when he got clean looks, and if Garcia's level change timing is off early, Hernandez has the power to change the fight in a single exchange. Round 1 or early Round 2 is when this is most plausible — once the fight grinds into later rounds, Garcia's conditioning advantage historically shows.

**#3 -- Rafa Garcia wins by TKO, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Garcia's only recent finish came in Round 3 against Jared Gordon, suggesting that accumulated damage late in fights can eventually lead to a stoppage. If Hernandez tires and his defense deteriorates in the third, Garcia — who has shown late-fight finishing ability — could push the referee's threshold. Not a primary path, but consistent with how Garcia's wins have looked when they don't go the scorecards cleanly.

**#4 -- Alexander Hernandez wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: This requires Hernandez to have genuinely corrected his defensive wrestling, either through improved footwork or a revised defensive game plan that keeps Garcia from establishing his pressure game. Given the first fight's lopsided grappling control, this outcome demands more adaptation than the evidence currently supports, but a rematch with full preparation time makes it at least theoretically live.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Davey Grant -- Style & Profile

- At 40 years old, Grant is the oldest fighter on the card, and the age cliff is real — bantamweights past 35 typically see meaningful physical decline, and Grant is five years past that mark. His last three fights tell a story of someone managing output, not generating it.
- His career striking numbers look respectable (5.58 significant strikes per minute, 45% accuracy), but his last-3 splits tell a completely different story: 2.93, 2.92, and a ghost-like 0.2 strikes per minute in those three fights. That is not a rounding error — Grant has become an extremely conservative, volume-suppressed fighter who wins on ring generalship and positioning rather than output.
- The Da'Mon Blackshear fight in particular is almost impossible to explain at face value: 0.2 significant strikes per minute across three rounds and he still won a unanimous decision. That is a grappling and cage-control performance, not a striking one — yet his takedown numbers in that same stretch are zero attempts. He is controlling distance and pace without engaging.
- His submission loss to Charles Jourdain in Round 1 is the most significant data point in his recent history. Jourdain is primarily a striker who caught Grant with a rear-naked choke — that suggests Grant was hurt, possibly dropped, and gave up the back. A fighter who gets submitted by a striker is not operating at full sharpness.
- His finishes have completely dried up. Of his last six wins, five have gone to decision. His two KO wins in the last ten fights came in 2022 (Martinez, Round 2) and one other; he is not a finishing threat at this stage of his career.
- Grant's win over Luna Martinetti already happened — this is an immediate rematch. Grant won by unanimous decision in April 2026, which is the most relevant data point of all.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Adrian Luna Martinetti -- Style & Profile

- Luna Martinetti's career UFC striking numbers are eye-catching in all the wrong directions: 8.80 significant strikes landed per minute but 9.30 absorbed. He is in a phone booth every time he fights, throwing and eating in equal measure — which sounds exciting until you factor in that his strike defense sits at just 47%. Nearly every other strike thrown at him lands.
- The volume number is almost certainly inflated by small sample size — he has only two UFC fights, and his last-3 splits show a dramatic compression: 2.87 strikes per minute against Vologdin, then just 1.2 against Grant in the loss. He was not able to impose his pace in the rematch opponent's first meeting.
- His 100% takedown defense career number is a fiction of sample size — with only two UFC bouts and no recorded takedown attempts against him completing, it is a placeholder, not a real data point. Do not read elite wrestling defense into that number.
- He is coming off a loss to the man he is now fighting for the second time. In the first meeting, he landed at 43.9% accuracy but only managed 1.2 significant strikes per minute — that is the output of a fighter who was being controlled, not one who was controlling. Grant physically neutralized his pace.
- At 17-2 overall with both losses coming in the UFC (one to Grant, one recorded), his regional record is the foundation of his confidence. But the step up in competition has exposed real defensive fragility — absorbing over 9 significant strikes per minute against UFC-level opposition is not a style quirk, it is a problem.
- He is 10 years younger than Grant, which matters over the course of a fight and is the most legitimate edge he carries into this rematch. If anyone in this fight is going to look fresher in Round 3, it is Luna Martinetti.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have the answer to this matchup — they fought four months ago and Grant won a clean unanimous decision. The question for the rematch is whether anything structural has changed, and the honest answer is: not much. Grant's approach of controlling distance, suppressing pace, and grinding out rounds was effective against Luna Martinetti's naturally high-volume but high-absorption style. Luna Martinetti threw nearly nothing (1.2 significant strikes per minute) and landed even less. Grant's experience and ring generalship allowed him to make a younger, more naturally active fighter look passive.

The core structural problem for Luna Martinetti is that he is a high-output pressure fighter whose output completely disappeared against Grant. His 47% strike defense means he gets touched consistently, and Grant — despite his own compressed volume — was accurate enough (49.4% in their first fight) to win the exchanges that mattered. Grant has a two-inch reach disadvantage, but at 40 years old he has been fighting defensively for years, using footwork and timing rather than range to control distance. Luna Martinetti cannot simply walk Grant down; the first fight proved that. Luna Martinetti's path in the rematch would require him to find a way to increase his output dramatically — to actually impose the pace that his career numbers suggest he is capable of — while absorbing less. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Raoni Barcelos -- Style & Profile

- Barcelos has reinvented himself as a pure grappling-control fighter in his current win streak. His last three fights produced SLpM figures of 0.2, 1.07, and 0.4 — he is not trying to strike his way to wins anymore. He gets takedowns, pins opponents down, and grinds out decisions.
- His takedown defense is elite at 87% career, which matters enormously in this matchup — it means he can choose when the fight is on the feet and when it goes to the mat, rather than being taken down against his will.
- At 38, he is well past the age cliff for a bantamweight, and his striking output reflects it. He threw 51 total significant strikes in his last three fights combined, which is near-historically low for a three-fight span. He is not a punching threat anymore — he wins by controlling where the fight happens.
- The Garbrandt win is the best recent résumé item — Garbrandt is a legitimate KO artist at the weight, and Barcelos neutralized him completely through wrestling and clinch control, winning every round on all cards. That is exactly the blueprint he will try to replicate here.
- He was stopped by Umar Nurmagomedov via TKO in Round 1 roughly two years ago — his only recent stoppage loss. Against elite top-end opposition he can be cracked, but he has not shown that vulnerability against the level of competition he has faced in his five-fight win streak.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Montel Jackson -- Style & Profile

- Jackson's best weapon is his reach — at 75 inches, he has an eight-inch advantage over Barcelos, and historically he used that to control range and land straight shots from the outside. His three early-round KO wins (all in Round 1) came from him exploiting that length before opponents could get inside.
- His last three fights tell a different story from his earlier career. He has combined for 0.58 SLpM across those three bouts with a strike accuracy range of 33–58%. He is not landing — and he is not using his wrestling, going 0 takedowns in his last two fights.
- His takedown defense at 67% is a real vulnerability. Against a wrestler like Barcelos who shoots often and accurately, that number says Jackson will be taken down at least some portion of the fight, and when he gets there, he rarely gets back up with conviction. His 0 takedown attempts in the last two fights suggest he is not threatening from the outside either.
- He is a southpaw, which creates natural alignment problems for orthodox opponents in the lead-leg and left-hand matchup — but that structural advantage only matters when he is actually engaging. His recent output suggests he is not comfortable engaging at high volume.
- Coming off consecutive split-decision losses — including the first meeting with Barcelos — Jackson needs a different answer than the one he brought to their first fight. His KO power is real but it has not shown up in any of his last three outings, and he is being bet up as a heavy favorite despite that trajectory.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The central tension in this rematch is whether Jackson can figure out what he could not figure out the first time: how to stop Barcelos from controlling the fight on the inside. Barcelos's entire game plan is to close distance, nullify the reach disadvantage, and either take Jackson down or grind him against the fence. His 87% takedown defense means Jackson cannot use wrestling as a counter — Barcelos gets to dictate terms. Jackson's 67% takedown defense means that when Barcelos does shoot, he is landing on more than one in three attempts. The first fight ended in three rounds of Barcelos controlling position and Jackson unable to generate anything consistent on the feet or in the clinch.

Jackson's realistic path to winning this rematch runs through his reach and power. If he can keep Barcelos at the end of his jab, use his southpaw left hand to keep Barcelos honest, and avoid getting dragged into clinch exchanges, he can make the fight uncomfortable for an aging, slow-starting Barcelos who barely throws on the feet anymore. The KO threat is genuine — Jackson has finished three opponents in Round 1 — but it requires him to land clean, which he has done almost none of recently. His output in the last three fights (0.27, 0.53, and 0.93 SLpM) suggests the finishing version of Jackson has not been present in the cage for over a year.

The reach gap of eight inches is real and cannot be dismissed — it is one of the largest differentials you will see in any UFC bantamweight fight. But Barcelos has already demonstrated in their first meeting that he can absorb the reach disadvantage by staying compact, moving forward, and removing Jackson's ability to use it. Jackson was unable to stay disciplined at range for three full rounds in their first encounter. The question is whether anything has changed in his game that would produce a different result, and from the available data, there is no evidence that it has.
analysis_sections.endings:

**#1 -- Barcelos wins by decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is literally what happened in their first fight and nothing in the data suggests a different outcome. Barcelos will grind, clinch, take Jackson down occasionally, and win the grappling and cage-control exchanges on the judges' cards. Jackson's recent output gives him no credible path to outscoring Barcelos on the feet across three rounds.

**#2 -- Jackson wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Jackson has legitimate power and eight inches of reach — if Barcelos gets reckless lunging in and eats a left hand on the way in, it ends quickly. Three of his last ten wins came by first-round stoppage. Barcelos walking into the pocket is the one scenario that wipes out every structural advantage he holds.

**#3 -- Barcelos wins by submission, Round 2 or 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Barcelos does threaten from top position and averaged 0.7 submission attempts per fifteen minutes across his career. He finished Cristian Quinonez by submission in Round 3 as recently as 2024. If he accumulates enough top time and Jackson fatigues from defending, a rear-naked choke or arm-in guillotine is possible — but Jackson has never been submitted in his career, so this requires sustained pressure.

The line movement shows both fighters' lines shortening in the same direction — Jackson going from +180 to -221 is an enormous swing with no reverse line movement detected, meaning the market moved with the public rather than against it.

The problem with the Jackson side at -221 is straightforward: Barcelos already won this fight. He did it by doing exactly what his stats say he does — low output, elite takedown defense, just enough wrestling and clinch work to win rounds. Target line: Barcelos at anything through +120 still carries value. At +150, the play is clear.

Stake recommendation: 1.5 units.

**Hedge / Arb verdict:** The best available line on Jackson is approximately -221 and Barcelos at +150 gives combined implied probability of approximately 40% + 69% = 109%. No arbitrage opportunity exists. Bet the value side only — Barcelos at +150.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight goes to decision" prop, if available at a reasonable number, warrants a look. Both fighters have gone to decision in all three of their most recent fights, including their first meeting. Neither has shown finishing output recently. If the decision prop is priced anywhere near -150 or longer on the YES side, it likely carries value.

**What would change this read:** If there is credible evidence that the first fight's judging was controversial and the scorecards were genuinely close rather than dominant for Barcelos, the edge narrows. If Jackson has made documented stylistic adjustments in camp — specifically improving his takedown defense or adding a clinch answer — that would also shift things. On the available data, neither condition is met.

This is a rematch where the market has swung dramatically from a roughly even-money fight to Jackson being a heavy favorite approaching -221, despite the fact that Barcelos just beat him three weeks ago (the layoff of "1 day" suggests this is a same-event rematch or the data is reflecting the immediate aftermath of their first fight). The line movement shows both fighters' lines shortening in the same direction — Jackson going from +180 to -221 is an enormous swing with no reverse line movement detected, meaning the market moved with the public rather than against it.

The problem with the Jackson side at -221 is straightforward: Barcelos already won this fight. He did it by doing exactly what his stats say he does — low output, elite takedown defense, just enough wrestling and clinch work to win rounds. Jackson produced 0.93 SLpM in their first meeting, took zero takedowns, and lost. At -221, the implied probability on Jackson is roughly 69%, and the de-vigged true probability sits around 66–67%. Nothing in the data — not the reach, not the power, not the southpaw angle — suggests Jackson is a 66% favorite over a man who just beat him.

**Recommendation: PLAY — Raoni Barcelos**

Assessed probability of Barcelos winning: approximately 57–60%. At the current line of +150, the implied probability on Barcelos is roughly 40%, and de-vigged it sits around 33–34%. That gap is meaningful and reflects a market that has dramatically overreacted to the first result in Jackson's favor — or is pricing Jackson's reputation and reach over what the fight tape actually showed.

Target line: Barcelos at anything through +120 still carries value. At +150, the play is clear.

Stake recommendation: 1.5 units.

**Hedge / Arb verdict:** The best available line on Jackson is approximately -221 and Barcelos at +150 gives combined implied probability of approximately 40% + 69% = 109%. No arbitrage opportunity exists. Bet the value side only — Barcelos at +150.

**Props worth considering:** A "fight goes to decision" prop, if available at a reasonable number, warrants a look. Both fighters have gone to decision in all three of their most recent fights, including their first meeting. Neither has shown finishing output recently. If the decision prop is priced anywhere near -150 or longer on the YES side, it likely carries value.

**What would change this read:** If there is credible evidence that the first fight's judging was controversial and the scorecards were genuinely close rather than dominant for Barcelos, the edge narrows. If Jackson has made documented stylistic adjustments in camp — specifically improving his takedown defense or adding a clinch answer — that would also shift things. On the available data, neither condition is met.
<!--END-->

## Ryan Spann -- Style & Profile

- Spann is a finishing machine when the fight goes his way — nine of his last ten UFC outcomes (wins and losses alike) have ended before the final bell, which tells you this is not a fighter who coasts to decisions. He wins by submission more than by knockout, but his last-3 splits show he can knock people out when the opportunity is there.
- His striking output is low by UFC light heavyweight standards — 3.11 significant strikes per minute career average, and his last three fights show that number trending even lower. He's not looking to out-volume opponents; he's waiting for openings and then capitalizing with grappling or a fight-ending exchange.
- His submission game is his most reliable weapon. He's landed submissions against OSP, Cutelaba, Brzeski, and Anthony Smith across his UFC run. His grappling rate spiked sharply against Brzeski (5.4 takedowns/15 min, 10.78 sub attempts/15 min), showing what he can look like when he commits to the ground.
- The chin has been tested. He's been stopped by KO four times in his last ten fights — Bogdan Guskov, Waldo Cortes Acosta, and now has a KO win in this very rematch. Prior to this fight, those losses were all in rounds 1-2, which points toward a chin issue rather than a cardio problem. He gets hit clean early and it's lights out.
- His takedown defense (59%) and takedown accuracy (34%) are both below average for the division, meaning he doesn't reliably control where the fight goes. He tends to get the best results when he can scramble into submissions rather than grinding top control.
- At 34, he's approaching the age range where light heavyweight fighters start declining physically, but nothing in his recent run shows a dramatic cliff yet — he's still finishing fights and getting finished, which at least means he's still explosive in both directions.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Marcus Buchecha -- Style & Profile

- Buchecha came into MMA as arguably the greatest competitive BJJ player of his generation, but his UFC run has been a difficult lesson in the gap between grappling pedigree and actual MMA effectiveness. He is 5-3-1 in the UFC with zero submission wins, and his last three fights — a draw and two losses — are a troubling pattern for someone who was supposed to be a ground-game nightmare.
- His takedown accuracy is alarming: 16% career in the UFC. He's attempting takedowns, but he's not completing them at anywhere near the rate his BJJ background would suggest. Against Nzechukwu he managed 2.0 takedowns per 15 minutes, but against Buday he had zero, and against Spann he attempted takedowns without finishing with submissions.
- His striking numbers are among the lowest in the division — 1.59 significant strikes per minute career average, and his last-3 average across the Nzechukwu and Buday fights was even lower. He's not a threat on the feet in any meaningful offensive sense, and when he can't get the fight to the ground, he has very little to fall back on.
- His strike defense (50%) is mediocre for a fighter who essentially has to eat punches to close distance and attempt takedowns. Against Spann in their first meeting, he absorbed enough to get knocked out in round two, which is the precise scenario his striking defensiveness invites.
- At 36 years old and with this level of recent performance — a draw with an unranked opponent and a loss to Martin Buday — his UFC career is at a crossroads. The question heading into a rematch is whether he can solve the specific problem that got him knocked out the first time, and so far nothing in his record suggests he's found an answer.
- His 0% takedown defense is not a typo — the UFC stats show no defensive wrestling recorded in his career. That makes him genuinely vulnerable to being taken down and controlled if an opponent wants to take the fight there, though most have been happy to stand with him instead.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

The first meeting already answered the central question of this matchup: Buchecha could not close the distance cleanly, ate a finish from Spann in round two, and never got the grappling exchanges he needed to deploy his BJJ. Rematch dynamics are real in MMA — fighters adjust — but Buchecha's adjustments are limited by what his striking game actually is. He lands 1.59 significant strikes per minute and absorbs 2.15, which means every striking exchange is a net loss for him. Spann is not a volume striker either, but his willingness to be patient and then uncork something damaging is the exact style that punishes slow-moving pressure fighters trying to close distance without meaningful striking.

The key exchange in this fight is whether Buchecha can survive long enough in the clinch or on the feet to land a takedown and work his grappling. His 16% takedown accuracy in the UFC says that's not a given — and his 66.7% striking accuracy against Spann in the first fight is actually respectable, which means he was landing when he threw. The problem is he wasn't throwing nearly enough (0.56 SLpM) to matter. Spann's grappling defense isn't elite (59% TDD), so if Buchecha gets desperate and shoots, there's a window there. But if Spann can stay upright and patient, his submission threat from scrambles and his knockout potential in stand-up exchanges both favor him.

The intangible picture here is straightforward: this is an immediate rematch where one fighter won by KO and the other was stopped. Fighters returning from knockouts — particularly ones finished in the first or second round — carry a measurable performance adjustment heading into the next fight, and Buchecha's overall trend line is pointing down rather than up. The line movement tells the same story the first fight did: money has moved toward Spann, not away from him, which is consistent with a sharp read that the rematch result is likely to mirror the original.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- Ryan Spann wins by KO/TKO, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: High
Why: The first fight ended this way, and Buchecha's path to a different result requires him to either close distance faster or absorb less damage on the way in — neither of which his recent striking numbers suggest is happening. Spann's patient, opportunistic striking found its moment once before, and Buchecha's mediocre strike defense (50%) gives him real exposure every time he tries to engage. An early-to-mid fight stoppage is the most plausible outcome by a significant margin.

**#2 -- Ryan Spann wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Spann's submission game is arguably more reliable than his KO threat. If Buchecha survives early and pushes into clinch range, Spann has shown repeatedly — against Brzeski, OSP, Cutelaba — that he can convert scrambles into finishes. Buchecha's own grappling identity means he's comfortable on the mat, but that works both ways when his opponent is the one controlling the scramble.

**#3 -- Marcus Buchecha wins by submission**
Likelihood: Low
Why: This is the only realistic path for Buchecha — get the fight to the ground, survive the scrambles, and tap Spann with elite-level BJJ. Spann's 59% takedown defense does give Buchecha an avenue, and if he can weather the stand-up phase and score a takedown, his ground game could theoretically take over. But his 16% UFC takedown accuracy and zero submission wins in nine UFC fights make this the longest of the plausible outcomes.

**#4 -- Fight goes to decision**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Given that their first fight ended in a round-two knockout and both fighters have almost no decision history in their recent runs — Spann went 0-for-10 in decisions in his last ten fights, and Buchecha has gone to the scorecards twice with a draw and a loss — the idea that this one goes all three rounds seems deeply out of character for both men. It's possible but would require an unusually cautious approach from Spann or an unusually effective defensive performance from Buchecha.

analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Eric McConico -- Style & Profile

- McConico is a southpaw pressure fighter who wins by making fights ugly and grinding opponents into deep water. His striking volume is modest — under 3 significant strikes per minute for his career — but he keeps his feet moving forward and forces exchanges on his terms rather than his opponent's.
- His reach advantage (77 inches at middleweight) is a genuine weapon. Four inches of additional reach lets him dictate range, land the first shot in exchanges, and punish opponents who try to close distance in a straight line.
- His last three fights tell a fragmented story. Against Brundage he was barely active (0.2 SLpM, 27.3% accuracy) but ground out a split decision. Against Susurkaev he was more engaged but got knocked out in the third round. Against Vieira in the rematch he was disciplined and accurate, landing 78.6% of his strikes at very low volume — a performance built on not getting wrestled.
- He has been knocked out twice in his recent fight history, once by Ruziboev in Round 2 and once by Susurkaev in Round 3. Both losses came by KO/TKO, and the Ruziboev finish was particularly early (0:33 of Round 2). There is a real vulnerability to sustained power in his recent record that the line needs to account for.
- Defensively, his strike defense sits at just 45% for his UFC career — meaning he absorbs shots at a rate that would concern any analyst putting him in with a finisher. His TD defense of 68% is functional but not elite.
- Against Vieira specifically, McConico showed he can build a fight plan around avoiding the takedown and using his reach to poke and move. It worked once. The question is whether it works again in an immediate rematch where Vieira has had time to adjust.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Rodolfo Vieira -- Style & Profile

- Vieira is a world-class jiu-jitsu practitioner whose entire UFC identity is built around getting the fight to the ground. All four of his UFC submission wins came via choke, and his career submission average of 0.9 per 15 minutes reflects a fighter who is constantly hunting for that finish the moment contact is made on the mat.
- The problem is getting there. His takedown accuracy of 22% is genuinely poor for a dedicated grappler — he shoots a lot and lands only about one in five. Against opponents who sprawl well and circle off the fence, he can look thoroughly ineffective.
- His striking defense (49% career) is a significant liability. He absorbs 4.28 significant strikes per minute — among the highest at middleweight — and his last three fights show he has not found a way to fix it. Against Bo Nickal he was knocked out in Round 3, and his striking output in recent fights has been minimal (0.56–1.0 SLpM), suggesting he knows striking is not his path to victory.
- The Nickal KO loss is worth examining closely. Nickal is himself a grappler rather than a career power striker, meaning Vieira was finished by a non-elite finisher in Round 3. Combined with an overall pattern of absorbing heavy volume, this raises genuine questions about how his chin holds up late in fights as he tires.
- His record shows a consistent pattern: when he gets the grappling going he finishes fights quickly and impressively; when he cannot get the fight to the mat, he loses decisions by being out-pointed standing. There is very little middle ground in his fight history.
- At 36 years old with five UFC losses, and having just lost to McConico by unanimous decision, the burden of proof is on Vieira to show he has solved the problems the first fight exposed.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, and the first fight tells us most of what we need to know about how this rematch plays out structurally. McConico beat Vieira by doing two things: he kept the fight standing by sprawling and circling away from Vieira's takedown attempts (Vieira landed only 4 takedowns per 15 minutes despite heavy attempts, and McConico survived), and he used his reach advantage to control distance at low volume. McConico landed 78.6% of his strikes in that fight — an astronomical accuracy figure — but only threw 0.73 per minute. That tells you he picked his spots carefully and refused to get pulled into exchanges that would benefit Vieira. Vieira's 76.9% accuracy on his own output reflects the same thing: both men were measuring, neither was engaging at volume, and McConico won the positioning battle.

The core structural problem for Vieira remains unchanged. His takedown accuracy of 22% means even when he is committed to getting the fight down, he misses three out of four attempts. McConico's 68% TD defense is workable enough against a 22% shooter — even when Vieira gets through, he has to maintain position against someone who has clearly prepared specifically to counter his game. Vieira's path to winning requires landing enough takedowns to either submit McConico or tire him out on the ground, but the first fight showed McConico is willing and able to grind through those attempts. Vieira has no reliable striking game to fall back on — he absorbed more and landed less in that fight, and his chin in the third round against Nickal is a red flag if McConico can keep things competitive deep into Round 3. McConico's own KO vulnerability matters less here because Vieira is not a striker — his power threat comes from ground-and-pound in top position, not from clean shots on the feet.

The four-inch reach advantage is a real structural edge for McConico in a fight that is likely to stay standing more than Vieira wants. McConico can maintain the outside and punish Vieira's entries, making every takedown attempt costly. Vieira needs to close that gap and change levels in a pattern he simply has not been able to execute consistently at this level. The line on this rematch has shortened significantly toward Vieira, but the first fight result and the underlying grappling-accuracy numbers suggest that move is driven by name recognition and Vieira's submission pedigree rather than a genuine recalibration of the matchup dynamics.
analysis_sections.endings:
## Most Likely Fight Endings

**#1 -- McConico wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: High
Why: This is exactly how the first fight went, and the structural reasons it played out that way — McConico's reach, his willingness to fight at low volume, Vieira's poor takedown accuracy — have not fundamentally changed. McConico circles, picks his shots from range, and survives the grappling exchanges long enough to bank rounds on the judges' cards.

**#2 -- Vieira wins by submission, Round 1 or 2**
Likelihood: Moderate
Why: Vieira's best and perhaps only realistic path to victory is getting McConico to the mat early, before McConico can establish range and rhythm. If he can drag McConico into a scramble in the first two rounds, his submission skills at close quarters are elite. The early rounds are when Vieira has the energy to chain his takedown attempts and the cardio to maintain top control — if he doesn't finish early, the fight gets much harder for him.

**#3 -- Vieira wins by unanimous decision, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: If Vieira has made genuine adjustments — pressuring earlier, using the fence more aggressively to cut off McConico's footwork — he could accumulate enough takedowns and ground time to win two of three rounds. He managed 4 takedowns per 15 minutes in the first fight, and a repeat of that with better control time could flip the scorecards.

**#4 -- McConico wins by TKO, Round 3**
Likelihood: Low
Why: Vieira showed he can be finished late in fights, and McConico's accuracy means when he does land clean shots they tend to count. If Vieira is pushing for a finish late and walks into a well-timed counter on the feet in Round 3, there is a puncher's-chance version of this outcome given Vieira's recent chin questions.

## Eric McConico -- Style & Profile

- McConico is a southpaw pressure fighter who wins by making fights ugly and grinding opponents into deep water. His striking volume is modest — under 3 significant strikes per minute for his career — but he keeps his feet moving forward and forces exchanges on his terms rather than his opponent's.
- His reach advantage (77 inches at middleweight) is a genuine weapon. Four inches of additional reach lets him dictate range, land the first shot in exchanges, and punish opponents who try to close distance in a straight line.
- His last three fights tell a fragmented story. Against Brundage he was barely active (0.2 SLpM, 27.3% accuracy) but ground out a split decision. Against Susurkaev he was more engaged but got knocked out in the third round. Against Vieira in the rematch he was disciplined and accurate, landing 78.6% of his strikes at very low volume — a performance built on not getting wrestled.
- He has been knocked out twice in his recent fight history, once by Ruziboev in Round 2 and once by Susurkaev in Round 3. Both losses came by KO/TKO, and the Ruziboev finish was particularly early (0:33 of Round 2). There is a real vulnerability to sustained power in his recent record that the line needs to account for.
- Defensively, his strike defense sits at just 45% for his UFC career — meaning he absorbs shots at a rate that would concern any analyst putting him in with a finisher. His TD defense of 68% is functional but not elite.
- Against Vieira specifically, McConico showed he can build a fight plan around avoiding the takedown and using his reach to poke and move. It worked once. The question is whether it works again in an immediate rematch where Vieira has had time to adjust.

<!--END-->

<!--F2_PROFILE-->
## Rodolfo Vieira -- Style & Profile

- Vieira is a world-class jiu-jitsu practitioner whose entire UFC identity is built around getting the fight to the ground. All four of his UFC submission wins came via choke, and his career submission average of 0.9 per 15 minutes reflects a fighter who is constantly hunting for that finish the moment contact is made on the mat.
- The problem is getting there. His takedown accuracy of 22% is genuinely poor for a dedicated grappler — he shoots a lot and lands only about one in five. Against opponents who sprawl well and circle off the fence, he can look thoroughly ineffective.
- His striking defense (49% career) is a significant liability. He absorbs 4.28 significant strikes per minute — among the highest at middleweight — and his last three fights show he has not found a way to fix it. Against Bo Nickal he was knocked out in Round 3, and his striking output in recent fights has been minimal (0.56–1.0 SLpM), suggesting he knows striking is not his path to victory.
- The Nickal KO loss is worth examining closely. Nickal is himself a grappler rather than a career power striker, meaning Vieira was finished by a non-elite finisher in Round 3. Combined with an overall pattern of absorbing heavy volume, this raises genuine questions about how his chin holds up late in fights as he tires.
- His record shows a consistent pattern: when he gets the grappling going he finishes fights quickly and impressively; when he cannot get the fight to the mat, he loses decisions by being out-pointed standing. There is very little middle ground in his fight history.
- At 36 years old with five UFC losses, and having just lost to McConico by unanimous decision, the burden of proof is on Vieira to show he has solved the problems the first fight exposed.

<!--END-->

## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

This is a rematch, and the first fight tells us most of what we need to know about how this rematch plays out structurally. McConico beat Vieira by doing two things: he kept the fight standing by sprawling and circling away from Vieira's takedown attempts (Vieira landed only 4 takedowns per 15 minutes despite heavy attempts, and McConico survived), and he used his reach advantage to control distance at low volume. McConico landed 78.6% of his strikes in that fight — an astronomical accuracy figure — but only threw 0.73 per minute. That tells you he picked his spots carefully and refused to get pulled into exchanges that would benefit Vieira. Vieira's 76.9% accuracy on his own output reflects the same thing: both men were measuring, neither was engaging at volume, and McConico won the positioning battle.

The core structural problem for Vieira remains unchanged. His takedown accuracy of 22% means even when he is committed to getting the fight down, he misses three out of four attempts. McConico's 68% TD defense is workable enough against a 22% shooter — even when Vieira gets through, he has to maintain position against someone who has clearly prepared specifically to counter his game. analysis_sections.f1_profile:
## Jackson McVey -- Style & Profile

- McVey is a high-volume striker who throws an extraordinary number of strikes — 6.51 per minute on average, and a jaw-dropping 9.4 in the rematch against Dumas specifically. He works at a pace that most middleweights simply aren't prepared to absorb.
- His striking accuracy is elite for the volume he generates. Connecting on 69% of significant strikes career-wide means he isn't just throwing to throw — he's finding the target consistently, which is a rare combination at this level.
- His defensive numbers are the central vulnerability in this profile. He absorbs only 3.37 strikes per minute, which sounds manageable, but his strike defense sits at 23% — meaning he eats roughly three out of every four shots thrown at him. He wins because he out-lands opponents badly, not because he avoids damage.
- He has been submitted in two of his last three fights, including losses to both Brunno Ferreira (a legitimate grappling threat) and Zach Reese. His submission average of 1.2 per 15 minutes suggests he does attempt submissions, but his own defensive wrestling is nonexistent — zero takedown attempts and zero takedown defense recorded in his UFC career.
- The one win in his recent three-fight stretch is the first meeting with Dumas, where he submitted him in R1 at 2:14. That result is the entire reason he's the current betting favorite in this rematch.
- He's a southpaw, which creates natural timing advantages against orthodox fighters, particularly in the left-hand lead and the cross-body kick. Against an orthodox fighter like Dumas, that stance edge is real.
analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Sedriques Dumas -- Style & Profile

- Dumas is a grinding decision fighter whose wins have come by controlling pace and volume over three rounds rather than finishing. Three of his four wins in his last ten fights went to decision — his game is about accumulation, not explosiveness.
- His striking output is low — under 2.0 significant strikes per minute career-wide — but his accuracy has been surprisingly clean in recent fights. He connected on 75% of his strikes in the first McVey fight despite losing, and 30% in the Zach Reese no-contest. He picks his shots carefully, which helps his durability numbers.
- Defensively, he absorbs only 2.64 strikes per minute and blocks 53% of shots. Those are solid numbers against most opponents — but they were completely overwhelmed when McVey was throwing nearly 10 strikes per minute in their first fight.
- He has been submitted three times in his last four completed fights: Donte Johnson (R2), Jackson McVey (R1), and Josh Fremd (R2). He has also been knocked out twice — by Michal Oleksiejczuk and Nursultan Ruziboev, both in the first round. The pattern of getting finished early, frequently, across multiple methods is the defining concern in his profile.
- His takedown defense is 33% career-wide — among the worst in the division. When opponents get him down, he cannot get back up, and when he's fatigued or stunned, he gives up his back quickly.
- His record over the last 12 months is 0-2-1 (NC): two submission losses and a no-contest. He has not won a fight since beating Denis Tiuliulin by decision more than a year ago. He is trending in the wrong direction.
analysis_sections.head2head:
## Head-to-Head: Strengths & Weaknesses

We already have a direct result to anchor this analysis: McVey submitted Dumas in 2:14 of Round 1 in their April 2026 fight. That result wasn't a fluke of position — it was preceded by a striking sequence where McVey was out-landing Dumas at a rate of nearly 10 strikes per minute, forcing Dumas to defend desperately before the submission found its way home. The structural problem for Dumas is that McVey's volume is simply beyond what his defensive game can manage. Dumas's 53% strike defense works fine against average UFC middleweights. It is completely insufficient against a fighter landing 7–9 times per minute with 69–72% accuracy.

The path McVey needs to win is essentially to replicate what he already did. He works forward with his left hand, controls distance well enough to stay off the fence, and buries opponents in strike volume until something opens up. His own grappling defense is terrible — zero recorded takedown defense — but Dumas has only 31% takedown accuracy and has not successfully controlled or submitted an opponent in recent memory. The more relevant grappling threat actually runs in the other direction: McVey has submissions and he has shown he can get them against Dumas specifically. Dumas's 33% takedown defense means McVey can also consider his own wrestling if he wants to, though historically he never does.

The one realistic path for Dumas is a significant stylistic adjustment — either smothering McVey in the clinch early before volume builds, or catching him with something clean in the pocket when McVey gets too comfortable walking in. McVey's 23% strike defense is genuinely exploitable, and if Dumas can land something significant early and shift momentum, the fight could look different than the first one. But Dumas has not shown the finishing instinct to capitalize on that kind of moment — his wins come by decision, not by taking advantage of a hurt opponent. The net intangible picture here marginally favors McVey: he's younger, he's coming off a win, and Dumas is on a multi-fight losing streak that includes two early submissions. The line movement from -150 to -210 in McVey's direction reflects real money landing on that read.

Likelihood: Low
Why: If Dumas somehow finds a way to neutralize McVey's early activity — clinching, wrestling into the fence, or simply surviving the R1 storm — this could go to cards. analysis_sections.f2_profile:
## Assuerio Silva -- Style & Profile

- Assuerio Silva is 51 years old and has not competed professionally in approximately 19 years — his last UFC fight was April 2007. That is not a typical layoff. That is nearly two decades away from the sport.

- He weighed in at 241 lbs for this fight, which is a heavyweight (or at least a very large light heavyweight) stepping into a fight against a 135-pound bantamweight. analysis_sections.head2head:

This matchup has a fundamental structural problem that makes meaningful analysis difficult: the fighters appear to exist in entirely different contexts. Michelle Montague is an active 32-year-old bantamweight at 135 pounds, undefeated in the UFC, with fights from 2025 and 2026 on record. The weight class disparity alone (135 lbs vs. 241 lbs listed weight) suggests this either involves a massive misrepresentation in the data or a matchup that makes no athletic sense under normal circumstances.
